name: Credit Union Of America
description_short: Credit unions are member-owned nonprofit financial cooperatives offering banking services like savings accounts, checking accounts, credit cards, and loans with a community-oriented mission.
description_long:
Credit unions are member-owned nonprofit cooperative financial institutions that have operated globally for over a century. They differ fundamentally from commercial banks in their ownership structure and governance model. Members of a credit union are simultaneously owners and customers, electing their board of directors through a democratic one-person-one-vote system regardless of account balance. This cooperative model originated in Central Europe and has expanded to serve 375 million members worldwide as of 2018, with over 100 million members added between 2016 and 2018.

Credit unions offer a comprehensive range of financial services equivalent to commercial banks, including share accounts (savings accounts), share draft accounts (checking accounts), credit cards, consumer credit, share term certificates (certificates of deposit), and online banking. However, membership is typically required to access these services—only members may deposit or borrow money. Credit unions operate as nonprofit institutions, meaning they reinvest surplus revenue back into member services rather than maximizing shareholder profits, allowing them to offer competitive rates and lower fees.

Credit unions distinguish themselves through their community-oriented mission and superior member service. Public trust in credit unions stands at 60% compared to 30% for large banks in the US. During the 2008 financial crisis, credit unions demonstrated greater stability, with only 3.6% of mortgages being subprime compared to 23.6% at commercial banks, and banks were 2.5 times more likely to fail. Small businesses report 80% higher satisfaction with credit unions than large banks, and American credit unions more than doubled lending to small businesses from $30 billion (2008) to $60 billion (2016), while overall small business lending declined by $100 billion during the same period.

Credit unions genuinely serve a different market segment based on cooperative principles rather than profit maximization. However, membership eligibility varies by institution based on geographic area, employment, or group affiliation, which can be a barrier to access. While their track record during financial crises and lending practices to underserved markets like small businesses is documented, individual credit union quality, services, and rates vary significantly depending on the specific institution.
pros: Member-owned cooperative structure ensures democratic governance with one-person-one-vote system regardless of account size
Significantly higher public trust at 60% compared to 30% for large commercial banks
Only 3.6% subprime mortgage lending during 2008 crisis vs. 23.6% at commercial banks, demonstrating prudent lending practices
Banks were 2.5 times more likely to fail during financial crises compared to credit unions
Doubled lending to small businesses from $30 billion (2008) to $60 billion (2016) while overall small business lending declined
Small businesses report 80% higher satisfaction with credit unions than large banks
Comprehensive banking services including savings, checking, credit cards, credit products, and certificates of deposit
cons: Membership eligibility often restricted by geography, employment, or group affiliation, limiting accessibility
Only members can deposit or borrow money, requiring membership enrollment before accessing services
Credit union asset sizes and services vary dramatically—ranging from volunteer operations with handful of members to institutions with hundreds of thousands of members
Quality and product offerings differ significantly across individual credit unions, making standardized recommendations difficult
Smaller credit unions may offer fewer technology features or branch locations compared to national banks
best_for: Community-oriented consumers seeking member-owned financial institutions with democratic governance
Small business owners looking for more personalized lending and higher approval likelihood than traditional banks
Individuals prioritizing customer service quality and institutional stability over profit-driven banking
Members of eligible groups (geographic areas, employers, associations) seeking cooperative financial alternatives
diagnosis: Credit unions are best suited for consumers who value community-oriented banking, member ownership, and demonstrated financial stability over profit maximization and convenience. The primary caveat is that membership eligibility and service quality vary significantly by specific institution, so individuals must verify eligibility with their local credit union and evaluate its particular offerings, rates, and technology features.
typical_results_timeline: Membership approval and account opening typically takes 1-3 business days. Loan decisions are usually faster than traditional banks.
